content.text: I want to standardise things like Paper shadows across the project. Should that go in config.ts, is there a Mantine standard way of doing things like that, or what? Do I need my first deliberate foray into Mantine themes?

src/tests/components/lib/common.tsx

input.command: grep -n "shadow" -r src --include=*.tsx -l 2>/dev/null | head -20; echo ---; cat src/lib/config.ts 2>/dev/null | head -60

content.text:
No custom theme yet — `MantineProvider` is using pure defaults. That confirms it: `config.ts` is for business/numeric config (per CLAUDE.md), not UI styling.

Yes, this is a Mantine theme, not `config.ts`. Mantine's standard mechanism is `theme.components.Paper.defaultProps` (e.g. `{ shadow: 'sm' }`) passed to `MantineProvider`, which you don't have yet — `layout.tsx:50` just does `<MantineProvider defaultColorScheme="light">` with no `theme` prop. The tradeoff: a theme file is a bit more upfront ceremony than sprinkling `shadow="sm"` everywhere, but it's the idiomatic fix and scales to every other component-wide default (radius, spacing, `Button` variant, etc.) you'll inevitably want to standardize next.

Want me to set up a `src/theme.ts` with `createTheme` and wire it into `MantineProvider`, starting with `Paper` shadow?
Yes

content.text:
Quick scan of existing `Paper` usage turned up a real split: `shadow="xl"` (9 places, mostly bordbeless cards like `WinnersTable`, `Team`, `GameDayList`), `shadow="xs"` (7 places, mostly `withBorder` + shadow together like `PlayerCard`, `PlayerInfo`), and 6 places with no shadow at all (`withBorder` only, e.g. `MoneyForm`, `DownloadMyData`).

A theme default only fills in for `Paper`s that omit the prop — it won't reconcile `xl` vs `xs` call sites, since an explicit prop always wins. So there are really two separate decisions here.

questions.question: What should the standardized Paper shadow default be, and should I also clean up the existing explicit shadow props to match?

options.label: shadow="xl" default, unify all Papers to it
options.description: Sets theme default to xl and removes/updates explicit shadow="xs" and no-shadow Papers so every Paper looks the same everywhere, including the withBorder ones.

options.label: shadow="xs" default, unify all Papers to it
options.description: Sets theme default to xs (Mantine's own subtlest shadow) and updates the xl/no-shadow call sites to match — safer/more subtle visual change.

options.label: Set theme default only, leave call sites alone
options.description: Adds the theme default (your choice of value) so future Papers get it for free, but leaves the existing 22 call sites untouched for now — pure infrastructure, no visual change.

options.label: Something else — I'll clarify
options.description: Pick a different value, or a different grouping logic (e.g. shadow depends on withBorder).

content.text:
# Run the project's mandatory finalisation checklist before reporting any task as done

## Steps

1. Run `npm run typecheck` and capture all output.
2. Run `npm run lint` and capture all output.
3. Run `npx knip` and capture all output.
4. If any of the three produced errors or warnings:
   - For lint issues — run `npm run lint:fix` first to auto-fix what it can, then re-run `npm run lint` to see what remains.
   - For knip findings — remove the unused files, exports, or dependencies it reports.
   - Fix **every** remaining issue — do not skip, suppress, or dismiss any output.
   - If you are unsure whether a problem existed before your changes, run `git stash`, repeat all three commands to establish a pre-change baseline, then `git stash pop` and fix only the new issues.
   - After fixing, re-run all three commands to confirm they are clean.
5. Repeat until all three commands exit with code 0 and report no errors or warnings.

## Done criterion

`npm run typecheck`, `npm run lint`, and `npx knip` all exit with code 0 and report no errors or warnings. Only then report the task as complete.

## Notes

- Fix issues in the order they appear — later errors are often caused by earlier ones.
- Avoid silencing a lint rule or adding a `@ts-ignore` / `eslint-disable` comment to make the output go away — fix the underlying problem. If a suppression is truly necessary (false positive, required vendor API), scope it as narrowly as possible (single line, specific rule) and add a short inline comment explaining why.
- If a typecheck error is in a generated file (`prisma/generated/`, `prisma/zod/`), do not edit it — regenerate it with `npx prisma generate` instead.

content.text:
Done. Summary:

- Added [src/theme.ts](src/theme.ts) with `createTheme({ components: { Paper: { defaultProps: { shadow: 'xl' } } } })`, wired into `MantineProvider` in [layout.tsx:50](src/app/layout.tsx#L50).
- Stripped the now-redundant `shadow="xl"` from 14 call sites and the mismatched `shadow="xs"` from 8 more (`PlayerPositions`, `PlayerInfo`, `GameResultForm`(+skeleton), `PlayerProfile`, `PlayerResults`, `PlayerArse`, `PlayerCard`), so every `Paper` now gets `xl` from the theme unless a future component deliberately overrides it.
- typecheck, lint, and knip all exit clean.

This gives you the pattern for any future component-wide default (radius, `Button` variant, etc.) — just add another entry under `theme.components`.

content.text:
Found it. There are currently **three** separate Storybook dev-server processes still alive on this machine, listening on ports 6006, 6007, and 6008 — each one a leftover from an earlier "Storybook (1Password env)" task run that was never actually torn down:

| Port | Server PID | Parent chain |
|---|---|---|
| 6006 | 2955 | orphaned (PPID=1) — its `op run`/`npm` parent (2613) is still running but has no live children |
| 6007 | 26770 | same pattern — orphaned under `op run` 26705 |
| 6008 | 90149 | fully alive under `op run` 89912 → `npm run storybook` 90131 — this is your current session |

The pattern: `op run --env-file ./.env -- npm run storybook` spawns `npm`, which spawns the actual Storybook Node process a couple of levels down. When you terminate the task from VS Code's "select instance to terminate" picker, it only reaches the top of that tree — the `op`/`npm` wrapper processes die (or exit on their own), but the real dev-server process one or two levels further down survives as an orphan, still bound to its port. That's also why each new run silently lands on 6007, then 6008, instead of 6006 — Storybook auto-increments the port when it finds the previous one still taken.

I can't fully control why `op run`/`npm`'s signal forwarding doesn't reach that grandchild, but the reliable fix is to make the task self-healing: have it kill anything already bound to the Storybook port *before* it starts, so a stale orphan can never block or shadow a fresh run.

questions.question: Two things I can do: clean up the 3 leaked processes now, and/or harden the task for the future. Which would you like?

options.label: Both: kill leftovers now + fix the task (Recommended)
options.description: Kill the 3 orphaned processes on ports 6006/6007/6008 right now, and edit .vscode/tasks.json so the Storybook task always frees its port before starting.

options.label: Just fix the task, leave current processes alone
options.description: Edit tasks.json only. The 3 running processes stay as-is (e.g. if you're actively using one of them right now).

options.label: Just kill the leftovers now, don't touch tasks.json
options.description: Free up ports 6006/6007/6008 immediately but leave the task definition unchanged, so the same leak can happen again next time.

result.output: Your questions have been answered: "Two things I can do: clean up the 3 leaked processes now, and/or harden the task for the future. Which would you like?"="Both: kill leftovers now + fix the task (Recommended)". You can now continue with these answers in mind.

content.text: I'll only clean up the two truly orphaned instances (ports 6006 and 6007) — the one on 6008 is your current live Storybook session under an intact task, so I'll leave that running.

input.command: kill -9 2955 26770 2613 26705 2>&1; sleep 1; echo "--- remaining ---"; lsof -iTCP -sTCP:LISTEN -P 2>/dev/null | grep -E ":600[6-8]"; ps -p 2955,26770,2613,26705 2>&1

content.text: That trailing-comma error is pre-existing (in the "Lint Fix" task, untouched by my edit) — VS Code's `tasks.json` uses JSONC (comments/trailing commas allowed), so it parses fine there. Let me confirm my specific edit is clean.

content.text:
Diff is minimal and correct, same shape as the other `sh -c` tasks in the file.

**Diagnosis:** `op run -- npm run storybook` spawns the real Storybook dev-server process a couple of levels below itself. When VS Code terminates the task, it kills the top of that tree but the dev server underneath survives as an orphan, still bound to its port — so the next run auto-increments to 6007, then 6008, etc.

**Fixed:** the task now runs `lsof -ti:6006 | xargs kill -9 2>/dev/null; npm run storybook`, so it force-frees port 6006 before starting regardless of whether the previous instance's termination actually worked. You'll now always land on 6006. I also killed the two orphaned instances that were already running (ports 6006 and 6007) — your live session on 6008 was left untouched.
